is a type of [local authority](/wiki/Local_authority "Local authority") in [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand") and the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"). Unitary authorities are responsible for all [local government](/wiki/Local_government "Local government") functions within their areas, or perform additional functions that elsewhere are usually performed by multiple tiers of local government.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Type of local government body that covers two levels of government

|  |  |
| --- | --- |

Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *Find sources:* ["Unitary authority"](https://www.google.com/search?as_eq=wikipedia&q=%22Unitary+authority%22) – [news](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=nws&q=%22Unitary+authority%22+-wikipedia&tbs=ar:1) **·** [newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Unitary+authority%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Unitary+authority%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Unitary+authority%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Unitary+authority%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(August 2017)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

A **unitary authority** is a type of [local authority](/wiki/Local_authority "Local authority") in [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand") and the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"). Unitary authorities are responsible for all [local government](/wiki/Local_government "Local government") functions within their areas, or perform additional functions that elsewhere are usually performed by multiple tiers of local government.

Typically, unitary authorities cover [towns](/wiki/Town "Town") or [cities](/wiki/Cities "Cities") that are large enough to function independently of a [council](/wiki/Council "Council") or other authority. An authority can be a unit of a [county](/wiki/County "County") or [combined authority](/wiki/Combined_authority "Combined authority").

## New Zealand

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Unitary_authority&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: New Zealand")]

In [New Zealand](/wiki/Local_government_in_New_Zealand "Local government in New Zealand"), a unitary authority is a [territorial authority](/wiki/Territorial_authorities_of_New_Zealand "Territorial authorities of New Zealand") (district, city or metropolitan area) that also performs the functions of a [regional council](/wiki/Regions_of_New_Zealand "Regions of New Zealand"). As of 2026[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Unitary_authority&action=edit), there are five unitary authorities. They are (with the year they were constituted): [Gisborne District Council](/wiki/Gisborne_District_Council "Gisborne District Council") (1989), [Tasman District Council](/wiki/Tasman_District_Council "Tasman District Council") (1992), [Nelson City Council](/wiki/Nelson_City_Council "Nelson City Council") (1992), [Marlborough District Council](/wiki/Marlborough_District_Council "Marlborough District Council") (1992), and [Auckland Council](/wiki/Auckland_Council "Auckland Council") (2010).[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)

The [Chatham Islands](/wiki/Chatham_Islands "Chatham Islands"), located east of the [South Island](/wiki/South_Island "South Island"), have a [council with its own special legislation](/wiki/Chatham_Islands_Council "Chatham Islands Council"), constituted (1995) with powers similar to those of a regional authority.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## United Kingdom

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Unitary_authority&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: United Kingdom")]

Each of the four [countries of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Countries_of_the_United_Kingdom "Countries of the United Kingdom") uses a different term to describe their unitary authorities. However, the [Office for National Statistics](/wiki/Office_for_National_Statistics "Office for National Statistics") uses the collective term 'unitary administration' to describe all local government areas which operate as unitary authorities.[[5]](#cite_note-ONS_Geography_Guide-5)

### England

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Unitary_authority&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: England")]

Main article: [Unitary authorities of England](/wiki/Unitary_authorities_of_England "Unitary authorities of England")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/Bournemouth%2C_Unitary_Authority_tree_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1038887.jpg/250px-Bournemouth%2C_Unitary_Authority_tree_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1038887.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bournemouth,_Unitary_Authority_tree_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1038887.jpg)

[Bournemouth](/wiki/Bournemouth "Bournemouth"): Unitary Authority tree. The tree on the left, on the concourse of the [Bournemouth Town Hall](/wiki/Bournemouth_Town_Hall "Bournemouth Town Hall"), was planted on 1 April 1997 to mark the occasion of Bournemouth council becoming a unitary authority on that day. This was part of the local government reorganisation of the late 1990s, when certain more urban districts were essentially separated from the relevant county council, with no services for Bournemouth residents now being carried out by [Dorset County Council](/wiki/Dorset_County_Council "Dorset County Council").

In [England](/wiki/England "England"), "unitary authorities" are those local authorities set up in accordance with the Local Government Changes for England Regulations 1994 made under powers conferred by the [Local Government Act 1992](/wiki/Local_Government_Act_1992 "Local Government Act 1992") to form a single tier of [local government](/wiki/Local_government_in_the_United_Kingdom "Local government in the United Kingdom") in specified areas and which are responsible for almost all local government functions within such areas. While outwardly appearing to be similar, single-tier authorities formed using older legislation are not *unitary authorities* thus excluding e.g. the [Council of the Isles of Scilly](/wiki/Council_of_the_Isles_of_Scilly "Council of the Isles of Scilly") or any other single-tier authority formed under the older legislation and not since given the status of a unitary authority.

This is distinct from the two-tier system of local government that still exists in much of England, under which local government functions are divided between [county councils](/wiki/County_council "County council") (the upper tier) and [district](/wiki/Non-metropolitan_district "Non-metropolitan district") or borough councils. Until 1996, two-tier systems existed in [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland") and [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales"), but these have now been replaced by systems based on a single tier of local government, with some functions shared between groups of adjacent authorities. A single-tier system has existed in [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland "Northern Ireland") since 1973.

For many years, the description of the number of tiers in UK local government arrangements has routinely ignored any current or previous bodies at the lowest level of authorities elected by the voters within their area such as [parish](/wiki/Civil_parish "Civil parish") (in England and Wales) or community councils; such bodies do not exist or have not existed in all areas.

### Northern Ireland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Unitary_authority&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Northern Ireland")]

Main article: [Local government in Northern Ireland](/wiki/Local_government_in_Northern_Ireland "Local government in Northern Ireland")

See also: [Category:Administrative divisions of Northern Ireland](/wiki/Category:Administrative_divisions_of_Northern_Ireland "Category:Administrative divisions of Northern Ireland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/NI11w.jpg/250px-NI11w.jpg)](/wiki/File:NI11w.jpg)

Districts of Northern Ireland

Northern Ireland is divided into eleven districts for local government purposes. In Northern Ireland local councils have no responsibility for education, road building or housing (though they do nominate members to the advisory [Northern Ireland Housing Council](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Housing_Council "Northern Ireland Housing Council")). Their functions include [waste and recycling services](/wiki/Recycling_in_Northern_Ireland "Recycling in Northern Ireland"), leisure and community services, building control and local economic and cultural development. Since their reorganisation in 2015 councils in Northern Ireland have also taken on responsibility for planning functions. The collection of [rates](/wiki/Rates_(tax) "Rates (tax)") is handled by the [Land and Property Services](/wiki/Land_and_Property_Services "Land and Property Services") agency.

### Scotland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Unitary_authority&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Scotland")]

Main article: [Local government in Scotland](/wiki/Local_government_in_Scotland "Local government in Scotland")

Local authorities in [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland") are unitary in nature but not in name. The [Local Government etc. (Scotland) Act 1994](/wiki/Local_Government_etc._(Scotland)_Act_1994 "Local Government etc. (Scotland) Act 1994") created a single tier of local government throughout Scotland. On 1 April 1996, 32 local government areas, each with a [council](/wiki/Subdivisions_of_Scotland "Subdivisions of Scotland"), replaced the previous [two-tier structure](/wiki/Regions_and_districts_of_Scotland "Regions and districts of Scotland"), which had regional, islands and district councils. [Comhairle nan Eilean Siar](/wiki/Comhairle_nan_Eilean_Siar "Comhairle nan Eilean Siar") (formerly the Western Isles Council) uses the alternative [Gaelic](/wiki/Scottish_Gaelic_language "Scottish Gaelic language") designation *Comhairle*. While the phrase "unitary authority" is not used in Scottish legislation (whether from the Scottish Parliament or the UK Parliament), the term can be encountered (used either descriptively or erroneously) in a few official publications[[6]](#cite_note-6) and in (usually erroneous) use by United Kingdom government departments.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

### Wales

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Unitary_authority&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Wales")]

Main article: [Local government in Wales](/wiki/Local_government_in_Wales "Local government in Wales")

Local authorities in [Wales](/wiki/Local_government_in_Wales "Local government in Wales") are unitary in nature but are described by the [Local Government (Wales) Act 1994](/wiki/Local_Government_(Wales)_Act_1994 "Local Government (Wales) Act 1994") as "[principal councils](/wiki/Principal_council "Principal council")", and their areas as [principal areas](/wiki/Principal_area "Principal area").[[8]](#cite_note-8) Various other legislation (e.g. s.91(1) [Environment Act 1995](/wiki/Environment_Act_1995 "Environment Act 1995")) includes the counties and county boroughs of Wales within their individual interpretations of the phrase "unitary authority" as an interpretive not a definitive description. In s.2 of the Act each council formed for a county is allocated the respective English and Welsh descriptions of "[County Council](/wiki/County_Council "County Council")" or "*Cyngor Sir*", each council formed for a [County Borough](/wiki/County_Borough "County Borough") is allocated the respective descriptions of "County Borough Council" or "*Cyngor Bwrdeistref Sirol*"; in all cases the shorter alternative forms "Council" or *"Cyngor"* can be used.

## Similar concepts in other jurisdictions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Unitary_authority&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Similar concepts in other jurisdictions")]

For broader coverage of this topic, see [Local government](/wiki/Local_government "Local government").

* In Canada, each province creates its own system of local government, including several analogous to unitary authorities:
  + In [Alberta](/wiki/Alberta "Alberta") and [Nova Scotia](/wiki/Nova_Scotia "Nova Scotia") there is *only* one level of local government in that province.
  + In [Ontario](/wiki/Ontario "Ontario") the term [single-tier municipalities](/wiki/Single-tier_municipalities "Single-tier municipalities") is used, for a similar concept.
* In Germany, [kreisfreie Stadt](/wiki/Kreisfreie_Stadt "Kreisfreie Stadt") (lit. 'circle-free city') is the equivalent term for a city with the competences of both the [Gemeinde](/wiki/Gemeinde_(Germany) "Gemeinde (Germany)") (municipality) and the [Kreis](/wiki/Districts_of_Germany "Districts of Germany") (district, literally *circle*) administrative level.
* In France, the city of [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris") works like a department council and a municipal council. The department councils of the two departments of [Corsica](/wiki/Corsica "Corsica") and of the region merged into a *collectivité territoriale*.
* In [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland"), a [City with powiat rights](/wiki/City_with_powiat_rights "City with powiat rights"), or "urban county", is a city which is also responsible for district (poviat) administrative level, being part of no other *powiat* (e.g. [Kraków](/wiki/Krak%C3%B3w "Kraków"), [Łódź](/wiki/%C5%81%C3%B3d%C5%BA "Łódź"), [Wrocław](/wiki/Wroc%C5%82aw "Wrocław"), [Poznań](/wiki/Pozna%C5%84 "Poznań")). In total, 66 cities in Poland have this status.
* In Taiwan, most cities have only one tier of local government. Unlike the three [county-administered cities](/wiki/County-administered_city "County-administered city") ([Chiayi](/wiki/Chiayi "Chiayi"), [Keelung](/wiki/Keelung "Keelung"), and [Hsinchu](/wiki/Hsinchu "Hsinchu")), they are independent of their surrounding county. [Special municipalities](/wiki/Special_municipality_(Taiwan) "Special municipality (Taiwan)"), with the exception of a few [mountain indigenous districts](/wiki/District_(Taiwan) "District (Taiwan)") within them, are also unitary.
* In the United States, there are several types of single-tier governments.
  + In the states of [Connecticut](/wiki/Connecticut "Connecticut"), [Rhode Island](/wiki/Rhode_Island "Rhode Island"), and much of [Massachusetts](/wiki/Massachusetts "Massachusetts"), county government has been abolished, and the municipalities (known as [New England towns](/wiki/New_England_town "New England town")) are the only governing tier below the state government.
  + Conversely, in [Hawaii](/wiki/Hawaii "Hawaii"), municipal government below the county level has been abolished, and the state’s counties are the only governing tier below the state government.
  + In [Virginia](/wiki/Virginia "Virginia"), all municipalities with *city* status are, by definition, independent from any county.
  + The [District of Columbia](/wiki/Washington,_D.C. "Washington, D.C.") has had no lower tiers of government since the [District of Columbia Organic Act of 1871](/wiki/District_of_Columbia_Organic_Act_of_1871 "District of Columbia Organic Act of 1871").
  + In several states, certain municipalities are either accounted as [independent cities](/wiki/Independent_city "Independent city") (e.g. [Baltimore](/wiki/Baltimore "Baltimore")) or [consolidated city-counties](/wiki/Consolidated_city-county "Consolidated city-county") (e.g. [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia") and [San Francisco](/wiki/San_Francisco "San Francisco")). In either case there is a single local authority within the territory of the municipality, though in the latter case there may be a technical legal distinction between the city and county governments.

Office for National Statistics. 24 August 2023. Retrieved 9 December 2023. "The term 'unitary administration' is used in this context to describe all local authority districts (LAD) that form a single tier of local government (that is, all UK local authority districts except for English counties and non-metropolitan districts). The term therefore covers unitary authorities (UA), metropolitan districts and London boroughs in England; UAs in Wales; council areas in Scotland; and local government districts (LGDs) in Northern Ireland. Note though that the term is not in common use and that it is a generic term, rather than one that reflects a specific geographic type."
